sentences.text: My name is Rose Zar.
Z-A-R.
Ro'ska ?] Guterman.
Ro'ska ?] R-O-- with an apostrophe on the top-- an S-- with an apostrophe-- K-A.
And Guterman, G-U-T-E-R-M-A-N.
No.
Shoshana.
No.
Raizel.
Yes.
I had another name, and I was called by the name [?
Wanda ?] [?
Gajda. ?] This was a Polish name, and it was spelled [?
W-A-J-N-D-A, ?] and capital [?
G-A-I-D-A. ?] I think I-- I messed up the spelling.
This-- I lived under this name from-- 1942 till 1945.
My birthday is July 27, 1923, I was born.
Now, I am 73 years old.
I was born in Poland, in a city, Piotrkow, P-I-O-T-R-K-O-W.
Yes, Piotrkow Trybunalski.
No.
At that time, it was in Radom district, or sometimes Lodz, because they always changed.
Lodz, yes.
My mother-- my mother, she was giving the tone in the family.
And she was a very sophisticated woman with a terrific taste.
Bella, B-E-L-L-A, I think so.
Yes, and she had terrific taste and terrific sense of color.
And she wanted everything just so.
My father, his name was Herman, H-E-R-M-A-N, Guterman.
And he was dark, he had dark hair.
And he was a soldier in the Russian Army.
And he was a gutsy man.
And when he had enough fighting, he ran away.
He was a deserter in the war-- after the war, I think 1916, or '17, something, at that time.
And he saw my mother someplace, and he fell in love with her.
And then they got married.
He doesn't immediately sound like the type that would attract her.
They sound somewhat different.
No.
Well, he was very handsome, this I will tell you.
Very, very handsome.
And my grandma went to Piotrkow to-- to check him out.
And my mother was staying in-- in-- in Warsaw.
And first my mother-- my grandma didn't like the [NON-ENGLISH].
You know, what it--
She didn't like it.
I don't know.
She had the old-fashioned values.
She was a widow.
And she wanted something else for her daughter.
But then she agreed, and they got married.
Yes.
It was a happy marriage.
And what else can I tell you?
From a child's point of view, I-- I'm going to say a horrible thing, now-- I like my father better.
Because I liked his stories, how he ran away, he was a deserter in the war.
And I-- I admired his gutsiness.
And in some way, I think that I did similar thing later in the war.
And my mother was a lady.
She spoke French.
And she came from a long-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --line of teachers.
And that's about it.
Yes.
My father took care of it.
He taught me how to climb fences, how to climb trees, and that's it.
I was a tomboy.
Oh, we had a store, a shoe store.
And Poles were working for us.
Because at that time, you couldn't order shoes from a factory.
You have to have shoemakers to-- to make the shoes for you, and then you were selling them.
So that's what he was doing.
We had a store in our city.
And I lived on [INAUDIBLE], number 11.
And our store was number 10, not far.
Everything in the same place.
Tell us a little bit about your house.
It must have been very elegant, hmm?
Well, I don't know.
I don't know if it was elegant.
It was a modest house.
There was lots of pictures on the walls, because my mother took care of those pictures.
And I remember, we had them-- one of those pictures saved my father being caught by the Germans.
Because we had on the wall a picture, I don't remember who painted this.
And it looked like two farmers are saying the evening prayer in the field.
And one day, the Germans came looking for the men, for my brother and for my father.
And I had light hair.
And my mother has blonde-reddish hair.
They came in and he said, in German, [SPEAKING GERMAN].
He saw this pictures, the prayer, he said, the Jews don't live here.
And he-- he just went out.
Yes.
No.
He was about three years younger than I.
And I had to--
Ben-- [?
Benjo, ?] Benjamin.
And he was blonde, good looking, with a short nose.
Me was the one who had a big nose.
But he had a little nose.
And he was full of mischief all the time.
Yes.
I had their little sister, and her name was Pola.
We called her [?
Polcha. ?] And when the war started, well, she was about to-- to start public school.
But there was no schools for Jewish children, so she couldn't go.
And what we did, I started to teach her in our house.
It was very dangerous.
And soon, her girlfriend wanted me to teach them too.
The mother said, Ro'ska, please take my daughter and teach her.
So I had 10 students who I'm teaching elementary school.
And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --that's about it.
Not really.
I think she just arranged, a few times, the windows.
That's all she did, nothing else.
Well, I will say no.
We-- we lit Shabbat candles every Friday.
We kept a kosher house.
All Jews kept-- kept kosher houses.
And-- But it-- it wasn't religious.
We observed all the holidays, and that's about it.
The store was closed, I want you to know.
There were only two stores in our city that were open on Sabbath.
When I was little I used to go with my father, and it was called the [?
Deutche ?] [?
Shul. ?]
Yes.
Then I used to go with him there.
No.
The big shul was the Great Synagogue.
And this is where the-- this was a real, Orthodox synagogue.
And the [?
Deutche ?] [?
Shul ?] was a little like a Conservative shul.
People were wearing hats.
And then for beards, they had little tiny goatees.
And that's about it.
And-- but also, the women were separated.
The women sat on the balcony.
First of all, I will tell you that we lived in the Jewish section of the city.
You could smell Friday night.
You could smell it, the soup, and-- and-- and-- and whatever was cooked there.
And then, we had to go for sabbath to take to the baker-- it was called a cholent.
It was made with potatoes and beans and barley, and was packed around with-- with newspapers.
We send it to the baker, and he put it in, and it was staying in the-- in the-- in his oven till the next morning.
And you could see, the next morning, all the young kids going and schlepping the cholent from-- from the baker.
And-- and now, other holidays, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] because we are now short before Hanukkah.
Hanukkah was not a big deal.
We lit the candles, the kids played with the dreidel and-- or some other card games.
And we ate potato pancakes, fried.
And we did receive, every day, a little Hanukkah gelt.
A little-- just some money.
But not like, big presents there were here, for the children.
Now, another holiday was Passover.
This was a big holiday.
The house was cleaned from top to bottom, and in every corner.
And matzohs were brought.
We made a special section to keep the matzot.
And there was never, ever any bread.
Before Passover, I think they-- we burned the chametz, the bread, outside.
And from like about three o'clock, you couldn't have any bread in your house.
Oh yeah.
And your father led it?
Yes.
Yes, he sang beautifully.
As a matter of fact, he was in the great synagogue's-- once in the choir, because the rabbi-- they were looking, it was a cantor, his name was [INAUDIBLE].
And he was looking for good voices.
And my brother-- my father wanted my brother to be in the choir.
So he practiced with him all the time, in the house.
And he sang beautifully.
And my brother had a nice voice.
It was maybe, for a boy, it is one of the nicest times that they have a-- a voice before they changed.
And they practiced.
My father practiced with him
Yes.
Yes.
It wasn't that much work.
I even learned to do that too.
[LAUGHS]
'The Rosh Hashanah.' But I'm not going to sing, because my father said that I have no voice, no voice.
Especially this, what it was, 'The Rosh Hashanah.' And--
First of all, Rosh Hashanah, two days.
And the children got, twice a year, new outfits.
For the fall, Rosh Hashanah, and for Passover, for the spring.
And Yom Kippur was a holiday that everybody fasted.
So like, I wanted to fast when I was little.
But my mother said, no, you're too young.
Then in our yard there was a sukkah.
A few neighbors got together and build the sukkah.
And for us, it was fun because we were carrying the food to the sukkah.
And the men-- not the women-- the men were eating in the sukkah.
We were just allowed to-- to bring the food inside.
Yes.
There was a mikvah, and the religious woman went to the mikvah.
And I don't know what else happened there.
Nm-mm.
OK.
Piotrkow Trybunalski, because the tribunals that were--
The courts were held in our city.
And also, the jails were in our city.
Heavy, heavy walls, were-- where they kept the jailers.
And that how-- that how it was known, Piotrkow Trybunalski.
But since I remember it-- this was maybe in 16th century.
Or-- yeah, probably.
Then we had the king's palace in our city.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't know, but it was always locked and you couldn't get in there.
And-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] --the people, we had friends.
Because my father's sister was living with her family in our city.
And my mother came from Warsaw, so she didn't have family in our city.
I really don't-- I don't remember the numbers.
But I can look it up.
Don't-- I always was horrible with numbers.
Horrible.
Yes.
Maybe half, or a third?
It was a goodly number, a goodly number.
Because we have so many shtiblachs and synagogues, and-- and the-- the Rabbiner, and little other rabbis, so we had plenty.
Well, I will only tell you, the first I remember, there was this [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], I don't know, fascist organization.
They were picketing Jewish stores.
And if Poles wanted to go on into the Jewish store, they said, don't buy by the Jew.
And the-- the customers were very intimidated.
This was like, maybe 1938 or '37.
And the other--
Mhm.
There were Jewish-- when I was in public school, there were separate school for Jewish children and separate schools for Polish children.
The schools were public schools.
But Jewish children learned the Jewish religion and Jewish history in their school.
And the Polish children, the priest used to come.
And they were teaching them the-- the catechism, which is then the Catholic interpretation of the Bible.
My--
Mhm?
My brother-- see, they changed the schools.
And the schools where the boys were supposed to go was very far, far out, in the city.
In the winter you have to walk.
And the winters were very harsh.
So my father says, why can't you go to the Polish school?
It is just like, two blocks from us.
And [?
Benjo ?] speaks beautiful Polish.
We won't have to be suffering.
So my brother was enrolled in the Polish public school.
And there were only two children-- Jewish children-- in the Polish public school, a girl and a boy.
He was an excellent student, my brother.
And before Passover, Easter, the priest used to come with the crucifix and with Jesus Christ, and pass around the crucifix and whatever.
And the kids have to kiss.
And when it came to my brother, my brother said-- passed it around to another one.
So the priest said like this, what are you, a communist?
Why don't you-- don't you kiss the crucifix?
And he said, no, I am a Jew.
And he was so mad, the priest.
He send him out, all the time, for-- for-- for catechism, to have a free hour.
Then he figured it like this, hey, I'm giving-- giving this kid a good time.
[LAUGHS] He doesn't have to be there.
So he called my brother back and says, like this, you're sitting on the lesson and you learn everything.
And when the other kids don't know, you answer.
You have to know.
He said that it was a kind of a punishment.
But it came handy, later on, for my brother, very much.
So he learned catechism.
And because he was a good student, so the kids in his class liked him.
He had a sense of humor.
But the older children, like the seven graders or the six graders, when the-- when the bell rang, they was throwing stones at him.
And one time a stone hit his head.
He had to have a couple of stitches.
And when my father went to the principal he said, like this, Mr.
Guterman, I love to have your son in-- in the class.
Because my father and my mother were members of the parents-teachers conference.
He said, it would be better if your son goes to the Jewish school.
And then we took him out, and he went to the Jewish public school.
No, I didn't.
I don't think that I was a fighting type at that time yet.
It-- it came later.
And I didn't have any Polish friends.
All our friends were Jewish kids.
You know whom we know-- knew as a Pole?
The-- how do you say, she was taking care of the-- of the street.
And she was a-- a--
Yeah.
So this was the only Polish person that-- that lived there, because she got a free apartment for that, and probably some money, too.
Yes.
Mhm.
OK.
See, my father-- and it's real funny here-- was a-- a socialist, a Bundist party.
He belonged to the Bund.
But I didn't want to go to the Bund.
I didn't like the Bund.
And I thought it is a good idea that the Jews will have a country on their own.
So I-- I had a neighbor.
And she was older, maybe 17 years old.
And she said, do you know what, Ro'ska?
You come with me to the club, to the Hashomer Hatzair.
You will make a good club member.
And I went there, and I just loved it.
We sang Hebrew songs.
We-- we-- we danced Israeli dances.
And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --well, it was just fun.
That's-- that's all.
I think I was 11 years old, or 10.
I cannot remember exactly, 10 or 11.
And it was fun.
Yes.
All my friends were in this Hashomer Hatzair youth group.
We had other youth groups.
But I belonged to that.
We had lectures.
And we had literature, how you call it?
Like, a judgment.
You took a book, and the one was the persecutor--
No, it was made-- it was made like a judgement.
Like a trial.
And I just loved it.
I really loved it.
And-- because it stimulated you mentally.
You had to read all those books that-- that they put on your list.
And it was fun.
Yes.
But I also learned Hebrew in gymnasium.
But I don't know, I wasn't very good at it.
Yes.
I thought that we will go to Israel.
And we had to work it out with my father.
Because see, I told you, he was a Bundist.
And at that time you have to go on Hashara.
You know, that you are prepared to this labor work, to work the-- the fields in the kibbutzim.
So I said to my father, I am going to go.
And my father said, go.
And-- and then you will send me a certificate, and I will come.
[LAUGHS]
Yes.
Yes, a lot.
You-- a happy life, laughter, friendships, little love encounters.
Just little, you know, flirtations.
And it was-- it was lots of fun.
Now, I had one boyfriend.
But I just didn't connect with him, you know?
Mentally.
He was handsome looking.
And then something happened, I don't remember what.
My-- my husband, Mayer, was also a member in this Hashomer Hatzair club.
And we were the biggest enemies.
Because I was-- he was a leading figure.
And I was leading among the girls.
So we fought.
And soon fights changed into-- into love.
And we both survived the war.
From all the members in our group, which were 45, only 3 survived, or 4.
One girl went to Russia.
And Mayer went to concentration camps.
I went on Catholic papers.
And I think one extra person came back from the Soviet Union.
So that's all.
Everybody else was killed.
Yes.
In my book, it is a combination, my father's influence, be gutsy, do, be risky.
Because he always told me how he-- he was-- he ran away.
He was a deserter from the war.
And-- and then the Hashomer Hatzair.
My-- mt name of my book is,
Yes, and my father said that the best way to survive is in the mouth of the wolf, which means under his nose, but watch not to be devoured.
